enrichment.short_description: wger is a free, open-source, self-hosted fitness platform for planning workout routines, tracking nutrition, and logging body measurements and progress photos.
enrichment.seo_title: wger Review 2026: Free Open-Source Workout and Nutrition Manager
enrichment.seo_meta_description: wger is a free, open-source fitness manager for workout routines, nutrition tracking, and progress logging. See features, self-hosting options, and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: wger (Workout GEneratoR) is a free and open-source fitness and workout management application originally built by developer Roland Geider and shared publicly in 2014. It is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL-3.0 or later), meaning the full source code is available and anyone can self-host, modify, or contribute to it, and there is no company or paid tier behind the core software. The project is maintained by the wger-project organization on GitHub with contributions from a broad community of developers and translators. wger covers three main areas of personal fitness tracking: workout planning, nutrition logging, and body progress monitoring. Users can build weekly training routines from a library of more than 100 exercises, each configurable with sets, reps, weight, duration, or distance targets, and the software supports automatic weight progression rules so routines adapt over time. For nutrition, wger connects to the Open Food Facts database, giving access to a food catalog of several million items, and automatically calculates energy, protein, carbohydrate, and fat totals for logged meals. Users can also track body weight, custom measurements, and upload progress photos to visualize change over time. A REST API is available for building integrations or automations on top of the platform, and wger includes basic multi-user and gym-management functionality, making it usable by individual trainees as well as small gyms or trainers managing several clients. wger is cross-platform: it ships official Android and iOS mobile apps (the Android app dates to 2020, the iOS app to 2024), and the Android build is also distributed through F-Droid and Flathub for users who prefer open-source app stores. Because it is self-hosted, deployment is typically done via Docker, giving administrators full control over their data, with no ads and no sale of personal information — a common selling point for people who want to leave closed, subscription-based fitness apps. Third-party managed-hosting providers (such as Elestio) offer wger as a hosted service for people who do not want to run their own server, but this is a separate commercial offering layered on top of the free open-source project rather than an official paid plan from the wger project itself. wger is best understood as a privacy-respecting, community-maintained alternative to commercial fitness-tracking apps like Strong, Hevy, or MyFitnessPal, aimed at users and small gyms who want full ownership of their training and nutrition data.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is wger
paragraphs: wger is a free, open-source fitness and workout management platform that lets users plan training routines, track nutrition, and log body progress without relying on a closed, subscription-based app.

The project began as a personal effort by developer Roland Geider and was shared publicly in 2014. It is licensed under AGPL-3.0, meaning the complete source code is open, self-hostable, and modifiable by anyone.

Because there is no company behind wger charging for access, the platform is maintained by an open community of contributors on GitHub, with translations, exercise data, and features added collaboratively over time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: wger combines workout planning, nutrition tracking, and body-progress logging in one system. Training routines can be built from a library of over 100 exercises with configurable sets, reps, weight, or distance targets, and support automatic weight-progression rules.
Nutrition tracking taps the Open Food Facts database, giving users access to a catalog of millions of foods with automatic calculation of calories, protein, carbs, and fat for logged meals.

Users can log body weight and custom measurements and upload progress photos, while a REST API supports building custom integrations. Basic gym-management and multi-user features make wger usable by trainers managing several clients, not just individuals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: wger is completely free to use as self-hosted, open-source software; there is no paid tier, license fee, or feature paywall from the project itself.
Most users deploy it via Docker on their own server, VPS, or home lab, giving them full ownership of their fitness and nutrition data with no ads and no data sold to third parties.

For people who do not want to manage their own server, third-party providers offer managed wger hosting for a fee, but this is a separate commercial service layered on top of the free project rather than an official wger pricing plan.

features.description: Design weekly training plans from a library of 100-plus exercises with custom sets, reps, weight, duration, or distance targets.

features.title: Automatic weight progression
features.description: Apply progression rules so routines automatically adjust load or volume across training cycles.

features.title: Nutrition and calorie tracking
features.description: Log meals against the Open Food Facts database of millions of foods with automatic macro and calorie calculation.

features.title: Body measurement and photo logging
features.description: Track body weight, custom measurements, and progress photos over time.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Build custom integrations or automations against wger data using the built-in API.

features.title: Basic gym management
features.description: Multi-user support lets trainers or small gyms manage several trainees from one instance.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Official Android and iOS apps plus F-Droid and Flathub distribution for open-source app stores.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Deploy via Docker for full control of the server and data, with no ads or data sharing.

pros: Completely free and open source with no feature paywalls
Full data ownership through self-hosting
Large exercise and food database for planning and logging
Active community contributing translations and features
REST API enables custom integrations
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge or a third-party managed host to get started
No official company support channel or SLA
Mobile apps and UI are less polished than some commercial fitness apps
Advanced gym-business features are more limited than dedicated gym-management software

faqs.question: Is wger free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. wger is free, open-source software licensed under AGPL-3.0, with no paid tiers from the project itself.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host wger
faqs.answer: The core project is self-hosted, typically via Docker, though third-party providers offer managed hosting for a fee if you would rather not run your own server.

faqs.question: What can I track in wger
faqs.answer: Workout routines and exercise progress, nutrition and calorie intake using the Open Food Facts database, and body weight, measurements, and progress photos.

faqs.question: Does wger have mobile apps
faqs.answer: Yes, official Android and iOS apps are available, with the Android build also distributed via F-Droid and Flathub.

faqs.question: Can trainers manage multiple clients in wger
faqs.answer: Yes, wger includes basic multi-user and gym-management functionality suitable for trainers or small gyms.

faqs.question: Is my data private in wger
faqs.answer: Since wger is self-hosted, you control where your data lives; the project states it does not use ads or sell personal information.

faqs.question: Does wger have an API
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API is available for building integrations or automations on top of your wger data.

faqs.question: Who maintains wger
faqs.answer: wger is maintained by the wger-project open-source community on GitHub, originally started by developer Roland Geider.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted personal training log
use_cases.description: Individuals who want a private, ad-free alternative to commercial fitness apps can self-host wger to track workouts, nutrition, and body progress.

use_cases.title: Small gym or personal trainer client management
use_cases.description: Trainers can run a shared wger instance to plan routines and monitor progress for multiple clients.
use_cases.audience: Independent personal trainers and small gyms

use_cases.title: Developer-extensible fitness platform
use_cases.description: Developers can use wger's REST API and open codebase to build custom fitness tools or integrate training data into other systems.

features: Nutrition tracking with Open Food Facts

enrichment.short_description: Whatfix is an enterprise digital adoption platform that layers in-app guidance, simulated training, and usage analytics onto web, desktop, and mobile applications to speed up onboarding and cut support tickets.
enrichment.headquarters: San Jose, California and Bengaluru, India
enrichment.seo_title: Whatfix Review 2026: Digital Adoption Platform Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Whatfix is an enterprise digital adoption platform for in-app guidance, training, and analytics. See features, plans, and how it compares for onboarding software.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Whatfix is an enterprise digital adoption platform (DAP) co-founded in 2014 by Khadim Batti (CEO) and Vara Kumar, headquartered jointly in San Jose, California and Bengaluru, India. The company has raised over 300 million dollars in venture funding, including a Series E round led by Warburg Pincus and previous backing from SoftBank Vision Fund 2, reaching a reported valuation of roughly 900 million dollars, and employs over a thousand people globally. Whatfix helps organizations overlay contextual, in-app guidance on top of any web, desktop, or mobile software so employees and customers can learn how to use complex applications without leaving the workflow. Its core product line spans four areas: the Digital Adoption Platform for web and desktop apps, a mobile SDK, an Operating System layer for Windows and Mac guidance, and Mirror, a tool for capturing screen recordings and turning them into guided simulations and training content, including SCORM exports for learning management systems. Within the DAP, features include a no-code editor and AI-assisted content authoring, task lists, smart tips, in-app flows, beacons, pop-ups, launchers, and an in-app wiki with AI-generated summaries. Enterprise governance features support content approvals, QA, rollback, and controlled releases, so large organizations can manage guidance content across many applications without breaking existing workflows. A companion Product Analytics module tracks user action tags, funnels, trends, and user journeys to show where employees or customers struggle, and Whatfix pairs this with adoption dashboards for continuous optimization. Common use cases include onboarding new hires and new customers, guiding employees through complex multi-step workflows such as CRM or ERP processes, deflecting help-desk tickets through in-app self-service, reinforcing compliance and process adherence, and supporting software rollouts, migrations, and policy changes. Whatfix competes with other digital adoption platforms such as WalkMe and Pendo, and it is typically deployed by large and mid-market enterprises layering guidance onto systems like Salesforce, Workday, SAP, or custom internal tools. Pricing is fully custom: Whatfix does not publish fixed prices and instead quotes based on the number of applications, modules, and user licenses involved, combining a flat platform fee with per-user licensing, and offering Standard, Premium, and Enterprise tiers plus optional add-ons like white-labeling, on-premise authoring, and dedicated adoption program management.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Whatfix
paragraphs: Whatfix is an enterprise digital adoption platform that overlays contextual, in-app guidance, simulated training, and usage analytics on top of web, desktop, and mobile applications.

Founded in 2014 by Khadim Batti and Vara Kumar, the company is headquartered in both San Jose, California and Bengaluru, India, and has grown into one of the larger vendors in the digital adoption category, backed by significant venture funding.
The platform is designed for large organizations that need employees and customers to learn complex software quickly, whether that is an internal ERP system, a CRM, or a customer-facing SaaS product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Whatfix's core Digital Adoption Platform provides a no-code editor and AI-assisted authoring for building task lists, smart tips, flows, beacons, pop-ups, and launchers directly inside any application.
Enterprise governance tools support content approval workflows, QA, rollback, and controlled release management, so guidance content can be maintained safely across many applications at scale.

Beyond the core DAP, Whatfix offers a mobile SDK, an Operating System layer for desktop guidance, and Mirror, a screen-recording tool that converts real workflows into simulations and SCORM-exportable training content, complemented by a Product Analytics module for funnel, trend, and user-journey insights.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Whatfix does not publish fixed public pricing. Instead, it quotes deployments individually based on the applications covered, the modules selected, and the number of user licenses.

The published tier structure includes Standard, Premium, and Enterprise plans across its Digital Adoption Platform, Product Analytics, Mobile, and Mirror products, with Enterprise plans supporting multi-app or unlimited implementations.

The overall pricing model combines a flat platform fee tied to the chosen product and plan with additional per-user license fees, and optional add-ons such as white-labeling, on-premise authoring, and 24/7 support are quoted separately.

features.title: Enterprise integrations and SSO
features.description: Supports SSO, IP whitelisting, data residency, and integrations with enterprise systems.

features.title: Self-service support deflection
features.description: In-app guidance and search reduce reliance on help-desk tickets by answering questions in context.

Strong enterprise governance for managing content at scale

AI-assisted authoring speeds up content creation
cons: No public pricing makes budgeting difficult upfront

Implementation and content maintenance require dedicated internal ownership

faqs.question: What does Whatfix do
faqs.answer: Whatfix is a digital adoption platform that adds in-app guidance, simulated training, and analytics on top of existing software so users can learn it without leaving their workflow.

faqs.question: How much does Whatfix cost
faqs.answer: Whatfix does not publish fixed pricing. Costs are quoted based on the applications covered, modules selected, and number of user licenses, combining a flat fee with per-user charges.

faqs.question: Who founded Whatfix
faqs.answer: Whatfix was co-founded in 2014 by Khadim Batti, who is CEO, and Vara Kumar.

faqs.question: Where is Whatfix based
faqs.answer: Whatfix is headquartered in both San Jose, California and Bengaluru, India.

faqs.answer: Mirror is Whatfix's tool for capturing screen recordings of real workflows and turning them into guided simulations and training content, including SCORM exports.

faqs.question: Does Whatfix work on desktop applications
faqs.answer: Yes, Whatfix offers an Operating System layer that provides in-app guidance and support for Windows and Mac desktop applications.

faqs.question: What is Whatfix Product Analytics
faqs.answer: It is a companion module that tracks user action tags, funnels, trends, and user journeys to show where employees or customers experience friction.

faqs.question: Who are Whatfix's main competitors
faqs.answer: Whatfix competes primarily with other digital adoption platforms such as WalkMe and Pendo.

enrichment.short_description: When I Work is an employee scheduling and time-tracking platform that combines shift scheduling, a mobile time clock, and team messaging in one app for shift-based businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: When I Work Review 2026: Employee Scheduling Software Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: When I Work is an employee scheduling and time-tracking app for shift-based teams. Compare Essentials, Pro, and Premium pricing, features, and integrations.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: When I Work is an employee scheduling and workforce management platform founded in 2010 by Chad Halvorson in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where the company remains headquartered. It was built to replace manual, spreadsheet-based shift scheduling for hourly and shift-based workplaces, and the company reports more than 200,000 workplaces and roughly one million users across over 50 countries using the platform. The core product centers on a drag-and-drop scheduling interface that supports fixed shifts, rotating shifts, multi-week schedules, and multi-location scheduling from a single account, along with reusable schedule templates and forecast tools for planning labor needs. Employees interact with the platform through a mobile app where they can view their schedule, submit availability, request time off, and initiate or accept shift swaps and OpenShifts (unfilled shifts employees can claim) without needing a manager to manually reassign work. A built-in time and attendance module lets employees clock in and out from mobile devices or physical kiosks, with geofencing to help confirm that clock-ins happen at the correct job site, and this time-tracking functionality can be toggled on or off across plans. Team messaging is built into the app as well, letting managers broadcast shift updates or communicate directly with staff without relying on a separate texting or messaging tool. When I Work integrates with more than 20 third-party systems, including Google Calendar, Gusto, Paychex Flex, ADP Workforce Now, QuickBooks Online, and Slack, which lets scheduling and time data flow into payroll and point-of-sale systems many businesses already use. Pricing is structured in three tiers billed per user per month: Essentials at 2.50 dollars covers unlimited users, auto scheduling, multi-week and multi-location scheduling, schedule templates, forecast tools, in-app messaging, OpenShifts and shift swapping, and payroll or POS integrations; Pro at 5 dollars adds advanced scheduling rules, role permissions, labor sharing, custom unit forecasting, custom reporting, and time zone toggling; and Premium at 8 dollars adds API key access, webhooks, and SAML/SSO for larger organizations that need deeper integration and identity management. All plans include a 14-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required, and customers can add or remove seats or cancel at any time without long-term contracts. When I Work is best suited for retail, hospitality, healthcare, and other hourly, shift-based businesses that need to build recurring schedules quickly and give frontline staff self-service control over their availability and time off.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is When I Work
paragraphs: When I Work is a scheduling and workforce management app built for hourly and shift-based businesses. It combines shift scheduling, time and attendance tracking, and team messaging into a single mobile-first platform.

Founded in 2010 by Chad Halvorson in Minneapolis, the company built the product to replace manual, spreadsheet-driven scheduling with a system where managers can build recurring schedules quickly and employees can manage their own availability.
The platform reports over 200,000 workplaces and roughly one million users worldwide, spanning industries like retail, hospitality, healthcare, and food service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scheduling is built around a drag-and-drop interface supporting fixed and rotating shifts, multi-week planning, multi-location scheduling, and reusable templates, backed by forecast tools for planning labor needs.
Employees use the mobile app to view schedules, submit availability, request time off, and manage shift swaps and OpenShifts, reducing back-and-forth with managers.

A time and attendance module supports mobile and kiosk clock-in with geofencing, and built-in team messaging lets managers communicate shift changes directly inside the app rather than through separate texting tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: When I Work offers three per-user, per-month plans: Essentials at 2.50 dollars, Pro at 5 dollars, and Premium at 8 dollars, each billed per user with no long-term contract required.

Essentials covers core scheduling, messaging, OpenShifts, and payroll or POS integrations. Pro adds advanced scheduling rules, role permissions, and custom reporting, while Premium adds API access, webhooks, and SAML/SSO for larger organizations.
A 14-day free trial with full feature access is available without a credit card, and customers can adjust seat counts or cancel at any time.

Mobile-first design suited to frontline, shift-based employees
Combines scheduling, time tracking, and messaging in one app
Broad set of payroll and POS integrations
14-day free trial with no credit card required
cons: Advanced features like API access and SSO are limited to the top Premium tier
Geared toward hourly, shift-based teams rather than salaried project work
Reporting and forecasting depth increases significantly only on paid tiers above Essentials
Some integrations require add-on configuration

faqs.question: How much does When I Work cost
faqs.answer: Plans are billed per user per month: Essentials starts at 2.50 dollars, Pro is 5 dollars, and Premium is 8 dollars per user per month.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes, When I Work offers a 14-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Who founded When I Work
faqs.answer: Chad Halvorson founded the company in 2010 in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where it remains headquartered.

faqs.question: Does When I Work include time tracking
faqs.answer: Yes, time and attendance tracking with mobile and kiosk clock-in and geofencing can be toggled on or off across all plans.

faqs.question: What integrations does When I Work support
faqs.answer: It integrates with over 20 tools including Google Calendar, Gusto, Paychex Flex, ADP Workforce Now, QuickBooks Online, and Slack.

faqs.question: Can employees swap shifts themselves
faqs.answer: Yes, employees can request time off and swap or claim OpenShifts directly in the mobile app without manager intervention for each change.

faqs.question: Does When I Work support multiple locations
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-location scheduling is included starting with the Essentials plan.

faqs.question: What businesses use When I Work
faqs.answer: It is designed for hourly and shift-based industries such as retail, hospitality, healthcare, and food service.

enrichment.short_description: Whereby is a browser-based video conferencing tool that lets people join meetings instantly via a link with no downloads, plus an embeddable video API for developers.
enrichment.starting_price: Free, paid plans from around $8.99 per user per month
enrichment.seo_title: Whereby Review 2026: Browser-Based Video Conferencing Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Whereby is a no-download, browser-based video calling tool with an embeddable video API. Compare Free, Pro, and Business plans and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Whereby is a browser-based video conferencing platform that originated as appear.in, a project started in 2013 as a summer intern effort inside Norwegian telecom company Telenor in Oslo. It was one of the earliest web applications to use the WebRTC standard, letting people join a video call directly from a browser link with no app download or account required for guests. In 2017 the product spun out with new ownership under Videonor, and in 2019 appear.in was rebranded to Whereby following a trademark dispute over the original name, along with a full UX overhaul of the meeting experience. The company operates as a distributed, largely remote organization with roots in Oslo, Norway. Whereby's product line has two main parts: Whereby Meetings, aimed at individuals and teams who want simple video calls, and Whereby Embedded, aimed at developers and product builders who want to add video calling into their own applications via API and SDK tooling. Meetings features include permanent, custom meeting room links that do not expire between calls, screen sharing, background effects, breakout groups, co-location groups for hybrid meetings, recording, and a virtual whiteboard, all accessible without installing dedicated meeting software. Whereby Embedded gives developers programmatic room creation, extensive customization options, and usage-based billing so companies can white-label video calling inside their own products rather than building WebRTC infrastructure from scratch. On the Meetings side, Whereby offers a Free plan limited to one meeting room, up to four attendees, and 30-minute session caps, alongside basic screen sharing and background effects. Paid Meetings plans (commonly marketed as Pro and Business tiers, with published pricing in roughly the 8 to 12 dollar per user per month range depending on the promotion and billing cycle) remove the time limit, add more room URLs, unlimited recording, custom branding, larger meeting capacity of up to around 200 participants, a custom subdomain, and priority support. Because Whereby is browser-first, it appeals to businesses that want to send external clients or candidates a simple video link without asking them to install software, which distinguishes it from heavier enterprise platforms like Zoom or Microsoft Teams. It is commonly used for client-facing meetings, recruiting interviews, telehealth-style consultations, and lightweight team calls, as well as being embedded directly into other SaaS products through its Embedded API.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Whereby
paragraphs: Whereby is a browser-based video conferencing tool that lets people join calls directly from a link, without downloading an app or creating an account as a guest.

The product started life as appear.in, launched in 2013 as an intern project inside Norwegian telecom Telenor in Oslo, and was one of the first web apps built on the WebRTC standard. It spun out with new ownership in 2017 and was rebranded to Whereby in 2019.

Whereby now offers two product lines: Whereby Meetings for individuals and teams who want simple, no-download video calls, and Whereby Embedded for developers who want to add video calling directly into their own applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Whereby Meetings offers permanent, custom room links, screen sharing, background effects, breakout groups, co-location groups for hybrid setups, recording, and a virtual whiteboard, all directly in the browser.
Whereby Embedded provides an API and SDK for developers to create meeting rooms programmatically and customize the video experience for their own products, billed based on usage rather than per seat.

Because it is browser-first, Whereby is often used for meetings involving external guests, such as client calls or interviews, where asking someone to install dedicated software would create friction.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Whereby offers a Free plan limited to one meeting room, up to four attendees, and 30-minute session limits, along with basic screen sharing and background effects.

Paid Meetings plans remove the session time limit and add features like more room URLs, unlimited recording, custom branding, larger participant capacity, and priority support, generally priced per user per month.
Whereby Embedded is billed separately on a usage basis, since it is aimed at developers embedding video calling into their own products rather than individual meeting hosts.

features.title: No-download browser meetings
features.description: Guests join video calls directly from a link in their browser with no app install required.

features.title: Permanent custom room links
features.description: Reusable meeting room URLs that do not expire, useful for recurring client or team meetings.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Paid plans support custom branding and a custom subdomain for a white-labeled meeting experience.

features.title: Whereby Embedded API and SDK
features.description: Developers can programmatically create and customize video rooms to embed calling inside their own applications.

features.title: Background effects
features.description: Blur or replace video backgrounds directly in the browser without extra software.

pros: No downloads needed for guests to join a call
Simple, permanent meeting links are easy to reuse and share
Embedded API lets developers add video calling to their own products
Clean, lightweight interface compared to heavier conferencing suites
Free plan available for casual or low-volume use
cons: Free plan is limited to four attendees and 30-minute sessions
Fewer enterprise-grade admin and compliance features than Zoom or Microsoft Teams
Larger meeting capacity requires upgrading to higher-priced plans
Embedded product requires developer resources to integrate

faqs.question: Do guests need to download an app to join a Whereby call
faqs.answer: No, Whereby is browser-based, so guests can join a meeting directly from a link without installing software.

faqs.question: Is Whereby free
faqs.answer: Yes, Whereby offers a Free plan limited to one meeting room, four attendees, and 30-minute sessions, with paid plans removing those limits.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Whereby Meetings and Whereby Embedded
faqs.answer: Meetings is the standalone video calling product for individuals and teams, while Embedded is an API and SDK for developers to add video calling into their own applications.

faqs.question: Was Whereby previously called something else
faqs.answer: Yes, Whereby was originally launched as appear.in in 2013 and was rebranded to Whereby in 2019.

faqs.question: Where is Whereby based
faqs.answer: Whereby traces its roots to Oslo, Norway, and operates as a largely remote, distributed organization.

faqs.question: How many people can join a Whereby meeting
faqs.answer: Capacity depends on the plan, with higher-tier plans supporting significantly more participants than the Free plan's four-person limit.

faqs.question: Can I record meetings in Whereby
faqs.answer: Yes, recording is available, with unlimited recording included on paid plans.

faqs.question: Does Whereby support custom branding
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans allow custom branding and a custom subdomain for meeting links.

use_cases.title: Client-facing consultations
use_cases.description: Businesses can send clients a simple meeting link for calls without requiring them to install any software.

use_cases.title: Recruiting interviews
use_cases.description: Recruiters can use permanent room links for candidate interviews with minimal setup friction for external participants.

enrichment.short_description: Whitespark is a local SEO software and services company providing citation building, Google Business Profile management, local rank tracking, and review management tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Whitespark Review 2026: Local SEO Citation Tools and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Whitespark offers local SEO software for citation building, Google Business Profile management, and rank tracking. Compare tools, services, and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Whitespark is a local SEO software and services company founded by Darren Shaw in 2005 and headquartered in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada. The company reoriented its focus specifically toward local search marketing around 2010 and has since become one of the more established, long-running names in local SEO tooling, known especially for pioneering local citation research. Whitespark's offerings split into two categories: self-serve software tools and done-for-you services. On the software side, the Local Citation Finder helps businesses discover where competitors are listed across local directories and data aggregators so they can build matching citations to strengthen local search relevance and trust signals. The Local Rank Tracker monitors keyword rankings specifically in local and map-pack results across mobile and desktop, letting agencies and businesses see how visibility changes by geography. Local Ranking Grids extend this by tracking rankings across up to 225 individual geographic grid points around a business location, which is useful for visualizing exactly where a business ranks well or poorly within its service area. The Local Platform tool focuses on Google Business Profile management, including bulk editing across multiple locations and safeguards against unwanted third-party updates to listing data. Reputation Builder automates review generation and monitoring, helping multi-location businesses collect and manage customer reviews at scale. On the services side, Whitespark offers a Listings Service for citation building and cleanup, a Yext Replacement Service aimed at businesses migrating away from Yext's subscription-based listings model, and broader done-for-you local SEO services and audits for businesses that want an agency to manage the work directly. Pricing is modular rather than a single bundled plan: the Local Platform starts at 1 dollar per month per location, Local Ranking Grids start around 10 dollars per month, the Local Rank Tracker ranges from about 14 to 200 dollars per month depending on tracked keywords and locations, the Local Citation Finder ranges from about 33 to 149 dollars per month, and Reputation Builder is about 79 dollars per month per location. One-time service pricing includes the Listings Service at 20 to 999 dollars and the Yext Replacement Service at 399 dollars per location, while done-for-you SEO services range from roughly 499 to 1,999 dollars per month. Enterprise and multi-location custom pricing is available across most products. Whitespark is best suited for local SEO agencies, multi-location businesses, and franchises that need to manage citations, rankings, and Google Business Profiles at scale, and it is frequently used alongside broader SEO platforms as a specialist local-search toolkit.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Whitespark
paragraphs: Whitespark is a local SEO software and services company that helps businesses and agencies improve visibility in local search and map results through citation building, Google Business Profile management, and local rank tracking.

Founded by Darren Shaw in 2005 and headquartered in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, the company shifted its focus specifically to local search marketing around 2010 and has since built a suite of tools known especially for local citation research.
Whitespark combines self-serve software with optional done-for-you services, so customers can either manage local SEO themselves or hand the work off to Whitespark's team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Local Citation Finder identifies where competitors are listed across local directories and aggregators, helping businesses build matching citations that strengthen local search relevance.

The Local Rank Tracker and Local Ranking Grids monitor keyword and map-pack rankings across mobile and desktop, with Ranking Grids tracking up to 225 geographic points to visualize exactly where visibility is strong or weak.

The Local Platform manages Google Business Profiles in bulk across locations and guards against unwanted third-party updates, while Reputation Builder automates review generation and monitoring for multi-location businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Whitespark uses modular, per-product pricing rather than one bundled plan. The Local Platform starts at 1 dollar per month per location, and other tools like the Local Rank Tracker, Local Citation Finder, and Local Ranking Grids scale from roughly 10 to 200 dollars per month depending on volume.
Reputation Builder is priced at about 79 dollars per month per location, with custom pricing for multi-location deployments.

Done-for-you services are priced separately: the Listings Service ranges from 20 to 999 dollars as a one-time fee, the Yext Replacement Service is 399 dollars per location one-time, and full-service SEO packages range from about 499 to 1,999 dollars per month.

enrichment.short_description: Whova is an all-in-one event management platform providing an award-winning attendee app, online registration, event marketing, and virtual and hybrid event tools for conferences and trade shows.
enrichment.seo_title: Whova Review 2026: Event Management App and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Whova is an all-in-one event management platform with an attendee app, registration, and virtual and hybrid event tools. See features, use cases, and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Whova is an event management platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in San Diego, California, built around an award-winning mobile event app alongside online registration, event marketing, and virtual and hybrid event tools. The company was started by a team including Soyeon Park, Weiwei Xiong, and Tianwei Sheng, with some sources also crediting founder Dr. YY Zhou, and it has grown to serve more than 50,000 events across over 170 countries. Whova's core product is the attendee-facing event app, which gives conference and trade-show attendees a personalized experience: customizable agendas, attendee profiles, in-app messaging, a community board for discussion, and a contact-exchange feature for digital networking. Session and engagement tools include live polling, Q&A, session attendance tracking, and a social wall that surfaces attendee-generated content in real time. For organizers, Whova provides comprehensive registration management, customizable event branding to match an event's theme, event badge generation, and real-time analytics on attendance and engagement. Sponsors and exhibitors get virtual exhibitor booths and lead-generation tools for capturing prospect information during the event, which supports Whova's use for trade shows and expos in addition to conferences. The platform also supports virtual and hybrid event formats, letting organizers run sessions online for remote attendees alongside or instead of an in-person component. Whova does not publish fixed public pricing; instead it charges a per-event fee that varies based on event size, expected attendance, duration, and which modules an organizer selects, such as the mobile app, registration tools, or virtual/hybrid event add-ons. Prospective customers typically request a custom quote or book a demo through Whova's sales team to get pricing tailored to their specific event. Because pricing and features scale with event complexity, Whova is used by a wide range of customers, from small professional association meetings to large multi-day industry conferences and trade shows, and it competes with other event technology platforms in the broader conference and event-app space. Its combination of attendee engagement tools, sponsor/exhibitor support, and organizer-side registration and analytics makes it a common choice for event planners who want one platform covering the full event lifecycle rather than stitching together separate registration, app, and networking tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Whova
paragraphs: Whova is an all-in-one event management platform built around a mobile event app, online registration, event marketing tools, and virtual and hybrid event support for conferences, trade shows, and similar gatherings.

Founded in 2013 and headquartered in San Diego, California, Whova has grown to serve more than 50,000 events across over 170 countries, spanning small professional meetings to large multi-day conferences.
The platform is designed to cover the full event lifecycle, from registration and marketing before the event to attendee engagement and analytics during and after it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The attendee-facing event app gives participants a personalized agenda, attendee profiles, in-app messaging, a community board, and a contact-exchange feature for digital networking, along with live polling, Q&A, and a real-time social wall.
Organizer tools include comprehensive registration management, customizable branding, event badge generation, and real-time analytics on attendance and engagement.
Sponsors and exhibitors get virtual exhibitor booths and lead-generation tools, and the platform supports virtual and hybrid formats so organizers can extend an event to remote attendees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Whova does not publish fixed public pricing. Instead, it charges a per-event fee that depends on factors like expected attendance, event duration, and which modules are selected.
Prospective customers typically request a custom price quote or book a demo through Whova's sales team to get pricing scoped to their specific event.
Because pricing scales with event size and complexity, costs can range widely between a small single-day meeting and a large multi-day conference with virtual or hybrid components.

enrichment.short_description: Wiki.js is a free, open-source, self-hosted wiki engine built on Node.js, offering Markdown and WYSIWYG editing, Git sync, and enterprise-grade authentication for team documentation.
enrichment.seo_title: Wiki.js Review 2026: Open-Source Self-Hosted Wiki Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Wiki.js is a free, open-source wiki engine for teams, with Markdown editing, Git sync, and self-hosted deployment. See features, editors, and setup options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Wiki.js is a free, open-source wiki engine created by developer Nicolas Giard under the Requarks project, with its first public release (version 1.0) arriving in 2016. It is licensed under the MIT license, meaning the full source code is freely available for anyone to inspect, self-host, or modify, and there is no paid tier from the project itself, though the maintainer accepts community support through channels like Patreon. Built on Node.js, Wiki.js is positioned as a modern, lightweight, self-hosted alternative to older wiki software such as MediaWiki, and it is frequently compared to tools like Confluence or Notion for teams that want to own their documentation infrastructure rather than rely on a hosted SaaS product. The editing experience supports multiple modes: a Markdown editor, a visual WYSIWYG editor, and a raw HTML mode, letting teams choose the workflow that suits different contributors. Authentication is a particular strength: Wiki.js ships with built-in local authentication including self-registration and password recovery, and it supports third-party login through Google, Facebook, Microsoft, GitHub, Discord, and Slack, as well as enterprise identity systems including LDAP, SAML, CAS, Auth0, Okta, and Azure AD, plus generic OAuth2 and OpenID Connect modules. This makes it viable for organizations that need to plug documentation into an existing single sign-on setup. Collaboration features include Git sync, which provides bidirectional synchronization between the wiki and a Git remote such as GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket, letting teams version-control their documentation content alongside code. Access control is granular, supporting page-level permissions with group-based rules so different teams can be restricted to relevant sections of the wiki. Search is flexible, with a built-in search engine as well as optional integration with Elasticsearch or Algolia for larger installations. On the database side, Wiki.js supports MariaDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and MS-SQL Server, giving administrators flexibility in how they run the backend. Deployment options include virtual machines, Docker, Kubernetes, and prebuilt cloud images for providers like DigitalOcean and AWS, and because it is self-hosted, organizations retain full ownership and control of their documentation data. Wiki.js is best suited for engineering teams, open-source projects, and organizations that want a modern, extensible, and fully self-hosted knowledge base without recurring SaaS subscription costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Wiki.js
paragraphs: Wiki.js is a free, open-source wiki engine built on Node.js, created by developer Nicolas Giard and first released publicly in 2016 under the Requarks project.
It is licensed under MIT, meaning the source code is fully open for self-hosting or modification, with no official paid tier from the project itself.

Wiki.js is commonly positioned as a modern, self-hosted alternative to older wiki software like MediaWiki, and to hosted documentation tools like Confluence or Notion, for teams that want to own their infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Wiki.js supports multiple editing modes, including Markdown, a visual WYSIWYG editor, and raw HTML, so different contributors can work the way they prefer.

Authentication is extensive, with built-in local login plus support for Google, Facebook, Microsoft, GitHub, Discord, and Slack, and enterprise systems including LDAP, SAML, CAS, Auth0, Okta, Azure AD, and generic OAuth2/OpenID Connect.

Collaboration features include bidirectional Git sync with GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket, granular page-level permissions with group-based rules, and full-text search with optional Elasticsearch or Algolia integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Wiki.js is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no official paid plan or feature paywall.

It supports MariaDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and MS-SQL Server as backend databases, and can be deployed via VM, Docker, Kubernetes, or prebuilt cloud images from providers like DigitalOcean and AWS.
Since it is self-hosted, the only ongoing cost is typically server or hosting infrastructure, which organizations control directly rather than paying a SaaS subscription.

Strong enterprise-grade authentication and SSO support
Git sync enables version-controlled documentation workflows
Flexible database and deployment options
Multiple editing modes suit different types of contributors
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge or infrastructure to deploy
No official managed hosting or paid support plan from the core project
Community-driven support rather than dedicated enterprise SLAs
Initial setup and configuration take more effort than a hosted SaaS wiki

faqs.question: Is Wiki.js free
faqs.answer: Yes, Wiki.js is free and open source under the MIT license, with no official paid tier.

faqs.question: Who created Wiki.js
faqs.answer: Wiki.js was created by developer Nicolas Giard under the Requarks project, with its first public release in 2016.

faqs.question: What databases does Wiki.js support
faqs.answer: Wiki.js supports MariaDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and MS-SQL Server.

faqs.question: Can Wiki.js sync with Git
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports bidirectional Git sync with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, or any Git remote.

faqs.question: Does Wiki.js support single sign-on
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports enterprise authentication including LDAP, SAML, CAS, Auth0, Okta, Azure AD, and generic OAuth2/OpenID Connect.

faqs.question: How do I deploy Wiki.js
faqs.answer: It can be deployed via virtual machine, Docker, Kubernetes, or prebuilt cloud images from providers like DigitalOcean and AWS.

faqs.answer: Markdown, a visual WYSIWYG editor, and raw HTML editing modes are all supported.

faqs.question: Is there a hosted version of Wiki.js
faqs.answer: The core project is self-hosted; third-party providers offer managed hosting separately, but there is no official Wiki.js-run SaaS plan.

enrichment.short_description: wildduck is an open-source, scalable IMAP and POP3 email server built on Node.js and MongoDB, designed for API-driven mail hosting and horizontal scaling.
enrichment.seo_title: wildduck Review 2026: Open-Source Scalable IMAP Email Server
enrichment.seo_meta_description: wildduck is a free, open-source IMAP and POP3 email server built for horizontal scaling. See features, architecture, licensing, and self-hosting details.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: wildduck is an open-source email server focused specifically on IMAP and POP3 mailbox access, created by developer Andris Reinman, who is based in Tallinn, Estonia and works as a technical architect at Zone Media. wildduck is part of the Zone Mail Suite (ZMS), a collection of related open-source mail server components, and it is licensed under the European Union Public License (EUPL) 1.2. Architecturally, wildduck is built on Node.js for the application layer and MongoDB for storage, and every mail-related object, including accounts, mailboxes, and messages, is stored in MongoDB with support for sharding. This design choice is deliberate: rather than relying on the traditional filesystem-based mailbox formats used by legacy mail servers, wildduck stores everything in a document database that can scale horizontally, and every wildduck instance is stateless, meaning administrators can run multiple instances behind a load balancer to increase throughput as account volume grows. The project explicitly targets larger deployments, describing itself as best suited for organizations managing 1,000 or more mailboxes that need horizontal scaling rather than the small, single-server setups more common with traditional IMAP servers like Dovecot. On the protocol side, wildduck provides a first-class implementation of IMAP4rev1, including support for IDLE (push-style new mail notifications), CONDSTORE (efficient synchronization of changes), COMPRESS, SPECIAL-USE folder flags, UTF8 support, and QUOTA management, along with full support for internationalized email addresses, folder names, and message headers, which matters for deployments serving non-English-speaking users. wildduck does not handle SMTP directly; instead, it is commonly bundled with Haraka for inbound SMTP and ZoneMTA for outbound SMTP to form a complete mail server stack, with wildduck itself handling the IMAP/POP3 mailbox-access layer. Management is handled entirely through a REST API covering accounts, mailboxes, messages, filters, auto-replies, DKIM key management, and more, which makes wildduck well suited for hosting providers or platforms that want to programmatically provision and manage large numbers of mailboxes rather than administering them by hand. Security features include support for application-specific passwords and multi-factor authentication, and notably, wildduck does not require root privileges and does not touch the underlying filesystem for mail storage, reducing its operational attack surface compared to traditional mail servers. As a fully open-source project, wildduck has no official pricing; it is free to self-host, and organizations bear only their own infrastructure and MongoDB hosting costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is wildduck
paragraphs: wildduck is an open-source IMAP and POP3 email server built on Node.js and MongoDB, created by developer Andris Reinman and released as part of the Zone Mail Suite under the EUPL 1.2 license.

Unlike traditional mail servers that store mailboxes on the filesystem, wildduck stores every account, mailbox, and message in MongoDB, which allows it to scale horizontally across sharded database clusters.

It is explicitly designed for larger deployments, targeting organizations managing 1,000 or more mailboxes that need to scale beyond a single server, and is commonly paired with Haraka and ZoneMTA to form a complete inbound and outbound mail server stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: wildduck provides a first-class IMAP4rev1 implementation with IDLE, CONDSTORE, COMPRESS, SPECIAL-USE, UTF8, and QUOTA support, along with full support for internationalized addresses, folder names, and headers.

Because every instance is stateless, administrators can run multiple wildduck instances behind a load balancer to increase throughput as mailbox volume grows, rather than scaling a single monolithic server.

The entire server is managed through a REST API covering accounts, mailboxes, messages, filters, auto-replies, and DKIM keys, and it supports application-specific passwords and multi-factor authentication without requiring root privileges.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: wildduck is free, open-source software licensed under the EUPL 1.2, with no official paid tier or license fee.
Since it is self-hosted, organizations only bear their own infrastructure costs, including servers and MongoDB hosting, rather than paying a subscription to a vendor.
There is no managed wildduck-as-a-service offering from the core project; deployment and scaling are handled by the organization running it.

features.title: REST API management
features.description: Manage accounts, mailboxes, messages, filters, auto-replies, and DKIM keys entirely through an API.

features.title: Internationalized email support

features.description: Security features for issuing scoped app passwords and enforcing multi-factor authentication.

features.title: Composable mail stack
features.description: Pairs with Haraka for inbound SMTP and ZoneMTA for outbound SMTP as part of the Zone Mail Suite.

pros: Free and fully open source under EUPL 1.2
Built for horizontal scaling to large mailbox counts
Comprehensive REST API for programmatic mailbox management
Strong protocol support, including internationalized email
Does not require root privileges, reducing operational risk
cons: Requires MongoDB expertise and infrastructure, unlike simpler file-based mail servers
Does not include SMTP handling on its own, requiring additional components like Haraka and ZoneMTA
Best suited for larger deployments, so it may be overkill for small personal mail setups
No official managed hosting or paid support option

faqs.question: What is wildduck
faqs.answer: wildduck is an open-source IMAP and POP3 email server built on Node.js and MongoDB, designed for scalable, API-driven mail hosting.

faqs.question: Is wildduck free
faqs.answer: Yes, wildduck is free and open source under the EUPL 1.2 license, with no official paid tier.

faqs.question: Does wildduck handle SMTP
faqs.answer: No, wildduck focuses on IMAP and POP3 mailbox access; it is typically paired with Haraka for inbound and ZoneMTA for outbound SMTP.

faqs.question: How does wildduck store mail data
faqs.answer: It stores accounts, mailboxes, and messages in MongoDB rather than on the filesystem, which supports sharding and horizontal scaling.

faqs.question: Who created wildduck
faqs.answer: wildduck was created by developer Andris Reinman, based in Tallinn, Estonia, as part of the Zone Mail Suite.

faqs.question: Is wildduck suitable for small personal mail servers
faqs.answer: It is explicitly designed for larger deployments of 1,000 or more mailboxes; smaller setups may be better served by simpler mail servers.

faqs.question: Can wildduck be managed via API
faqs.answer: Yes, all management, including accounts, mailboxes, filters, and DKIM keys, is handled through a REST API.

faqs.question: Does wildduck support multi-factor authentication
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports application-specific passwords and multi-factor authentication.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise email
use_cases.description: Organizations wanting full control of their email infrastructure can self-host wildduck alongside Haraka and ZoneMTA for a complete mail stack.

use_cases.title: SaaS products embedding mailbox features
use_cases.description: Developers building products that need managed mailbox infrastructure can integrate wildduck's REST API rather than building IMAP support from scratch.

features: REST API for accounts, mailboxes, and messages
Composable with Haraka and ZoneMTA for full mail stack
